---
title: "Journal Entry 4: Chapters 1 and 2 Reflections"
date: "2026-03-01"
categories: [reflection, writing, introduction, related-work]
description: "Reflections on writing the Introduction and Related Work chapters"
---

![Writing reflection](../thumbnail.jpg)

## Reflections on Writing Chapters 1 and 2

### Chapter 1: Introduction

#### Writing Process

Writing the introduction required me to balance technical detail with accessibility. I started by articulating the core problem: while version histories exist in Google Docs and similar platforms, they are difficult to parse without manual review. The challenge was explaining this problem in a way that felt urgent and important without assuming readers were already familiar with the pain points.

I decided to structure the introduction with multiple motivations—personal, professional, and research-oriented—to show that this problem manifests across different contexts. This required reflection on my own experiences with group projects and recognition of broader workplace coordination challenges.

#### Key Content Decisions

For background information, I included:
- The shift toward asynchronous collaboration in distributed teams
- The limitations of existing version control systems (Git vs. Google Docs)
- The gap between raw version data and human-readable understanding

The contributions section clearly articulated three key aspects of CoTrace:
1. Automated semantic summarization of changes
2. Contributor detection and impact measurement  
3. Fair, transparent contribution assessment grounded in ethical principles

#### Challenges and Solutions

The most challenging part was articulating _why_ this matters beyond convenience. I worked through several drafts to make clear that contribution measurement affects real outcomes (grades, promotions, recognition) and that fair assessment requires both technical sophistication and ethical grounding.

I received feedback that the ethical implications section was a strength, helping readers understand that this is not just a technical tool but an instrument that affects how people are evaluated and recognized. This feedback validated my decision to make ethical considerations central rather than peripheral.

### Chapter 2: Related Work

#### Literature Review Process

Finding relevant sources required searching across multiple domains:
- Version control and change tracking (Git, Google Docs literature)
- Contribution measurement in software engineering
- Automated text summarization and NLP
- CSCW (Computer-Supported Cooperative Work)
- Fairness and measurement ethics
- Academic integrity and writing process analysis

I used a combination of Google Scholar, ACM Digital Library, and domain-specific searches. The selection criteria included: relevance to collaborative document analysis, recency (prioritizing 2015+), and foundational works that established key concepts.

#### Organization Strategy

I organized the related work into thematic sections rather than chronological or tool-based groupings:
- Systems that provide complete but raw information (Git)
- Systems that prioritize accessibility over depth (Google Docs)
- Contribution measurement approaches
- Semantic summarization and NLP advances
- Fairness and ethical measurement

This organization helped show that no existing tool integrates all the necessary components—the gap CoTrace fills.

#### Key Insights from Literature

The most surprising finding was how extensively the limitations of quantitative contribution metrics have been documented. Researchers have known for years that commit counts, lines of code, and similar metrics are poor proxies for actual contribution value, yet no integrated solution exists for non-technical collaborative domains.

I also discovered the concept of "altmetrics" and fairness-aware measurement design, which significantly shaped how I positioned CoTrace's multi-metric approach.

### Overall Reflections

These two chapters work together by establishing both the urgency of the problem (Introduction) and the maturity of the technology landscape (Related Work). The Introduction asks "why should we care?" while Related Work answers "why doesn't something already solve this?"

Based on feedback from professors, I made several revisions:
- Deepened the ethical implications discussion
- Added concrete examples of contribution measurement failures
- Expanded coverage of asynchronous collaboration challenges
- Strengthened the synthesis showing how different tools address different aspects

Through writing these chapters, I learned that good academic writing requires moving beyond describing what exists to articulating _why current solutions are inadequate_. The related work isn't just a literature review—it's a structured argument for why new research is necessary.
